visual_checks: Check if the page content adapts to both portrait and landscape orientations
Look for any content that is cut off or inaccessible in one orientation
Check for CSS or JavaScript that locks the page to a specific orientation
Look for meta viewport tags or CSS media queries that restrict orientation
Verify the layout reflows appropriately when orientation changes
pass_conditions: Content is viewable and operable in both portrait and landscape orientations
No content is restricted to a single display orientation unless a specific orientation is essential
The page does not use CSS or JavaScript to force a single orientation
Layout adapts appropriately when the device is rotated
fail_conditions: Page content is locked to portrait-only or landscape-only orientation via CSS (transform: rotate) or JavaScript (screen.orientation.lock)
Content is only usable in one orientation — rotating causes content to be hidden, overlapped, or inaccessible
A message tells users to rotate their device without providing the content in the current orientation
CSS media query orientation locks hide content in one orientation
na_conditions: Content has an essential orientation requirement (e.g., a piano keyboard app, a check deposit requiring landscape for the check image, a VR application)
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots were only captured in one orientation and the other cannot be verified
The application is desktop-only and orientation testing is not applicable
common_mistakes: Using screen.orientation.lock('portrait') in JavaScript without an essential reason
CSS that uses transform: rotate(90deg) to force landscape content back to portrait
Displaying a 'Please rotate your device' overlay that blocks all content in one orientation
Mobile apps wrapped in a WebView that lock orientation at the native container level

examples.pass: A banking app shows account information that reflows properly from a single column in portrait to a wider layout in landscape, with all content accessible in both
examples.fail: A game website forces landscape orientation using JavaScript — when a user in a wheelchair mount has their tablet fixed in portrait, they see a 'Rotate device' message and cannot access any content
examples.partial: The page works in both orientations but a data table in landscape orientation has columns cut off that are visible in portrait, making some data inaccessible

plain_meaning: Content must work in both portrait and landscape orientation -- it must not be locked to one just because the developer assumed a particular device posture. The intent is to support users whose device is physically mounted in a fixed orientation (wheelchair-attached tablets, for example). The exception is when a specific orientation is genuinely essential to the content (e.g., a piano keyboard app, a check-deposit camera view).
scope_applies_to: Responsive web pages intended for mobile/tablet viewing
Pages or apps that currently lock orientation via CSS, JavaScript, or viewport meta
Pages that display a 'please rotate your device' blocker
Web apps that use screen.orientation.lock or similar APIs
scope_does_not_apply_to: Desktop-only pages with no meaningful mobile layout
Content where a specific orientation is essential (piano apps, bank check imaging, some AR experiences)
Reflow / zoom issues -- that belongs to SC 1.4.10
Text resize -- that belongs to SC 1.4.4
Pages that simply render differently in portrait vs landscape without locking -- that's responsive design, not a failure
borderline_calibration: A page that renders but is awkward in one orientation is not a failure -- only LOCKING the orientation fails.
A 'rotate your device' blocker on a non-essential page is a hard failure at medium+.
If you cannot test orientation in the harness, flag as needs-manual-verification rather than failing.
Essential-orientation claims require justification; do not accept them by default.

An orientation media query that only adjusts layout (font-size, width, padding) is NOT an orientation restriction. Only transform:rotate(90deg) or similar rotation forcing, or content that becomes unusable in one orientation, constitutes a 1.3.4 failure.
auditor_anti_patterns: FALSE POSITIVE: claiming CSS transform:rotate exists in an orientation media query without quoting the exact CSS rule from the programmatic data. Many orientation media queries only set font-size, display, or width — do NOT assume transform:rotate is present. Only flag orientation-locking if you can cite the EXACT CSS rule from the code data.
